title: Hello and Basic Courtesy
objective: Greet politely, thank and apologise, respond to thanks, and handle simple yes/no in a friendly first encounter.

teachingFrame.openingHook: The first five seconds of meeting someone in Thailand set the whole tone. With just a few polite words, you can open any conversation warmly.
teachingFrame.scenario: You meet staff or a new person for the first time and need to sound friendly, respectful, and calm.
teachingFrame.learnerTakeaway: By the end, you can greet, thank, apologise, respond to thanks, and answer yes or no politely.

sections.heading: Your first Thai word: สวัสดี
sections.purpose: Introduce สวัสดี as a standalone word — the universal Thai greeting — before combining it with anything else.
spokenNarration: Let's start with the very first word you will ever say in Thai. Listen first: สวัสดี sà-wàt-dii.
This one word covers hello, goodbye, good morning, good evening — all of it. สวัสดี is your safe greeting at any time of day.
Say it with me now: สวัสดี. One more time: สวัสดี. Good.
Imagine you walk into a shop in Bangkok. The first thing you say is สวัสดี. Simple, warm, and clear.
onScreenBullets: สวัสดี | sà-wàt-dii | hello
drills: Listen and repeat: สวัสดี sà-wàt-dii. Say it three times.
Pause-and-produce: you walk into a hotel lobby. What do you say? Say it aloud before I show the answer.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show สวัสดี as a single large triplet card — the learner's first Thai word.
teachingVisuals: Large Thai script สวัสดี centered in the left panel with transliteration and English below.
Clean background with no distracting imagery — the word itself is the star.
teacherCues: Model the word slowly and clearly before explaining it.
Use a warm tone — this is the learner's very first Thai word.

imageSupport.rationale: The greeting word itself is the teaching target. A photo would add clutter without helping recall.

sections.heading: Polite endings: ครับ and ค่ะ
sections.purpose: Teach ครับ and ค่ะ individually as standalone polite endings, then combine with สวัสดี into polite greeting chunks.
spokenNarration: In Thai, you finish almost every polite sentence with a small word that signals respect. Let's learn two of them now.
First: ครับ khráp. Listen: ครับ. This is the male polite ending. If you are a male speaker, you add ครับ after your sentences. Say it with me: ครับ.
Next: ค่ะ khâ. Listen: ค่ะ. This is the female polite ending for statements. If you are a female speaker, you add ค่ะ after your sentences. Say it with me: ค่ะ.
Think of ครับ and ค่ะ as the polite full stop at the end of your sentence. Without them, you sound blunt. With them, you sound friendly and respectful.

Now let's combine. You already know สวัสดี. Add ครับ: สวัสดีครับ sà-wàt-dii khráp — that is a polite hello from a male speaker. Add ค่ะ: สวัสดีค่ะ sà-wàt-dii khâ — a polite hello from a female speaker.
onScreenBullets: ครับ | khráp | polite ending (male)
สวัสดีครับ | sà-wàt-dii khráp | hello (male, polite)
สวัสดีค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ | hello (female, polite)
drills: Listen and repeat: ครับ khráp. Now: ค่ะ khâ. Repeat each three times.
Substitution drill: I say สวัสดี — you add ครับ. Now I say สวัสดี again — you add ค่ะ.
Your turn: I point to male or female. Say the correct full greeting: สวัสดีครับ or สวัสดีค่ะ.

languageFocus.thai: สวัสดีครับ

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show ครับ and ค่ะ as individual cards first, then the combined polite greeting chunks.
teachingVisuals: Two individual triplet cards for ครับ and ค่ะ side by side, labeled male/female.
Below: two combined greeting cards showing สวัสดีครับ and สวัสดีค่ะ.
teacherCues: Teach each polite ending individually before combining.
Model ครับ first, then ค่ะ, then build the chunks.

imageSupport.rationale: Polite endings are abstract function words. A clean contrast board teaches better than any image.

sections.heading: Thank you and sorry: ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ
sections.purpose: Teach ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ individually, then combine each with ครับ/ค่ะ into polite chunks.
spokenNarration: Now two more high-value words. Listen first: ขอบคุณ khàawp-khun. This means thank you.
ขอบคุณ. Say it with me: ขอบคุณ. You will use this one constantly — after help, after kindness, after service. Someone holds the door? ขอบคุณ.
Next word. Listen: ขอโทษ khǎaw-thôot. This means sorry or excuse me.
ขอโทษ does two jobs. It opens a gentle interruption — excuse me — and it repairs a small mistake — sorry. Need to get past someone? ขอโทษ. Bumped into someone? Also ขอโทษ.
Now combine them with your polite endings. ขอบคุณครับ khàawp-khun khráp — thank you, politely. ขอโทษค่ะ khǎaw-thôot khâ — sorry, politely. You can mix any combination.
onScreenBullets: ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | thank you
ขอโทษ | khǎaw-thôot | sorry / excuse me
ขอบคุณครับ | khàawp-khun khráp | thank you (male, polite)
ขอโทษค่ะ | khǎaw-thôot khâ | sorry (female, polite)
drills: Listen and repeat: ขอบคุณ khàawp-khun. Now: ขอโทษ khǎaw-thôot. Say each three times.
Discrimination drill: I describe a situation. You choose ขอบคุณ or ขอโทษ — say it aloud. Situation 1: someone helps you carry a bag. Situation 2: you need to pass through a crowd.
Substitution drill: say ขอบคุณ with ครับ. Now with ค่ะ. Say ขอโทษ with ครับ. Now with ค่ะ.

languageFocus.thai: ขอบคุณครับ

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ as individual triplet cards, then their polite forms.
teachingVisuals: Individual triplet card for ขอบคุณ — large and clear.
Individual triplet card for ขอโทษ — with note about dual meaning (sorry + excuse me).
teacherCues: Teach each word individually before combining with polite endings.
Explain the dual meaning of ขอโทษ with two quick scenarios.

imageSupport.rationale: These are abstract social function words. Text-based triplet cards teach the meanings most clearly.

sections.heading: Respond naturally: ไม่เป็นไร, ใช่, ไม่ใช่
sections.purpose: Teach ไม่เป็นไร, ใช่, and ไม่ใช่ individually so the learner can respond to thanks, apologies, and yes/no situations.
spokenNarration: You can now greet, thank, and apologise. But what happens when someone thanks or apologises to you? You need responses. Let's learn three.
First: ไม่เป็นไร mâi bpen rai. Listen: ไม่เป็นไร. This means it is okay, no problem, never mind. Learn it as one whole phrase.
Someone says ขอบคุณครับ to you? You say ไม่เป็นไร. Someone says ขอโทษค่ะ? You also say ไม่เป็นไร. It smooths the moment warmly. Say it with me: ไม่เป็นไร.
Next: ใช่ châi. Listen: ใช่. This means yes, that is right. It confirms something is correct. Say it with me: ใช่.
And: ไม่ใช่ mâi châi. Listen: ไม่ใช่. This means no, that is not right. It tells someone they are mistaken. Say it with me: ไม่ใช่.
Important: ใช่ and ไม่ใช่ answer the fact — is it right or not? ไม่เป็นไร answers the feeling — do not worry about it. Different jobs.
onScreenBullets: ไม่เป็นไร | mâi bpen rai | it is okay / no problem
ใช่ | châi | yes / that is right
ไม่ใช่ | mâi châi | no / not right
drills: Listen and repeat: ไม่เป็นไร mâi bpen rai. Say it three times. Now: ใช่ châi. Three times. Now: ไม่ใช่ mâi châi. Three times.
Response-building drill: I say ขอโทษครับ — what do you say back? Pause and answer. I say ขอบคุณค่ะ — what do you say back?
Pause-and-produce: is this สวัสดี? ใช่ or ไม่ใช่? Say it aloud before I show the answer.

languageFocus.notes: Teach as a whole chunk. Natural response after ขอบคุณ or ขอโทษ.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show three response words individually, then a mini-exchange pattern demonstrating when to use each one.
teachingVisuals: Three individual triplet cards for ไม่เป็นไร, ใช่, and ไม่ใช่.
A mini-exchange: ขอโทษครับ → ไม่เป็นไร. ใช่ไหม? → ใช่ / ไม่ใช่.
teacherCues: Teach each response word individually before showing exchange patterns.
Emphasise the difference: ใช่/ไม่ใช่ answers the fact, ไม่เป็นไร answers the feeling.

imageSupport.rationale: Response patterns are clearest as text-based exchange ladders, not photographs.

sections.heading: Pronunciation beat: hearing Thai tones
sections.purpose: Train the learner's ear to notice tone differences using words from this lesson, focusing on the high tone vs falling tone contrast.
spokenNarration: Before we roleplay, let's train your ear. Thai is a tone language — the pitch of your voice changes the meaning of a word.
Listen to two words you already know. ครับ khráp — that is a high tone. Your voice rises sharply upward. Now: ค่ะ khâ — that is a falling tone. Your voice starts high and drops down.
Try it yourself. Say ครับ with your voice going up. Now say ค่ะ with your voice starting high and dropping. Can you hear the difference?
Here is another contrast. Listen: ใช่ châi — falling tone, voice drops. Now: ขอบคุณ — the first syllable khàawp has a low tone, voice stays down low. Low tone and falling tone feel different.
Do not worry about mastering all five Thai tones today. Just notice: pitch matters in Thai. We will practise more tones every lesson.
onScreenBullets: ครับ | khráp | high tone — voice rises sharply
ค่ะ | khâ | falling tone — starts high, drops down
ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | low tone on khàawp — voice stays low
drills: Tone echo drill: I say ครับ khráp — repeat it. Now I say ค่ะ khâ — repeat it. Feel the difference in your voice.
Minimal pair choice: I say two words. Which one is the high tone ครับ and which is the falling tone ค่ะ? Point before I tell you.

languageFocus.notes: Pronunciation focus: high tone rises sharply upward.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show tone direction arrows next to the transliteration to visualise pitch movement.
teachingVisuals: Two large cards: ครับ khráp with upward arrow, ค่ะ khâ with downward arrow.
Simple pitch direction indicators — up for high tone, down for falling tone.
teacherCues: Model each tone slowly and clearly with exaggerated pitch.
Use hand gestures to show pitch direction — up for high, down for falling.

imageSupport.rationale: Tone arrows and pitch direction indicators teach better than any photograph.

roleplay.scenario: You approach a reception desk at a hotel in Bangkok. Greet the staff, confirm something, thank them, and handle a small exchange politely.

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: สวัสดีครับ

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ขอโทษครับ นี่โรงแรมสยามใช่ไหมครับ
lines.translit: khǎaw-thôot khráp nîi roong-raem sǎ-yǎam châi mái khráp
lines.english: Excuse me, is this the Siam Hotel?

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ขอบคุณครับ

lines.speaker: Staff
lines.thai: ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: สวัสดีครับ

recap: How do you say hello politely as a male speaker? Say it aloud.
Someone helps you. What do you say? Add a polite ending.
Someone apologises to you. What is the warm Thai response?
What is the difference between ใช่ and ไม่เป็นไร?
Say sorry politely as a female speaker.

pronunciationFocus.mouthMapAnchor: High tone: your voice rises sharply upward, like emphasising 'Really?!' in English. Falling tone: your voice starts high and drops down firmly, like saying 'No!' with conviction.
